# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**"What is meant by semi-conservative DNA replication? Name the classical experiment that proved it."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

DNA replication is called **semi-conservative** because each of the two daughter DNA molecules produced consists of **one parental (old) strand** and **one newly synthesised strand**. The parental strands act as templates, and each is preserved (conserved) in a daughter molecule.

The classical experiment that proved this was conducted by **Matthew Meselson and Franklin Stahl in 1958** using *Escherichia coli* and isotopic labelling with $^{15}\text{N}$ and $^{14}\text{N}$, followed by **CsCl density-gradient centrifugation**.

> **Mark Distribution:**
> - Definition of semi-conservative: 1.5 marks
> - Mention of Meselson–Stahl experiment: 1 mark
> - Key technique (isotopic labelling + CsCl): 0.5 mark

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**"List any three differences between the leading strand and the lagging strand during DNA replication."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Leading Strand | Lagging Strand |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synthesised **continuously** in one long piece | Synthesised **discontinuously** as short Okazaki fragments |
| 2 | Requires only **one RNA primer** | Requires **multiple RNA primers** (one per Okazaki fragment) |
| 3 | Synthesis proceeds **towards** the replication fork | Synthesis proceeds **away** from the fork, in 5′→3′ direction |

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per correct difference × 3.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Main Paper]**

**"Describe in detail the mechanism of DNA replication in *Escherichia coli*. Add a note on the enzymes involved."**

#### Part (a) — Stepwise Mechanism of Replication (7 Marks)

The replication of DNA in *E. coli* proceeds through the following orderly stages:

**1. Initiation (2 Marks):**
The **DnaA** initiator protein binds to four 9-bp **DnaA-box** sequences within the **oriC** region (245 bp). With ATP bound, DnaA oligomerises and melts the adjacent **AT-rich 13-mer repeats (DUE)**, exposing single-stranded DNA. The **DnaB₆–DnaC₆** helicase loader complex delivers **DnaB helicase** onto each separated strand, after which DnaC is released. This creates **two replication forks** that proceed bidirectionally, generating a **replication bubble**.

**2. Elongation (3 Marks):**
- **Leading strand:** A single **RNA primer** is laid down by **DnaG primase**. **DNA Polymerase III** then continuously extends the primer in the **5′→3′ direction**, using the parental 3′→5′ strand as template. The **β-clamp** tethers the polymerase, providing high processivity (~1000 nt/s).
- **Lagging strand:** Primase repeatedly synthesises short RNA primers. Pol III extends each into a short **Okazaki fragment** (~1–2 kb). On encountering the previous primer, Pol III releases and re-binds a new primer nearer the fork — this is the **trombone model**.

**3. Termination (1 Mark):**
Forks meet at the **Ter** region bound by **Tus** protein. **Topoisomerase IV** decatenates the two daughter duplexes, yielding two identical circular ds-DNA molecules.

**4. Primer Removal and Joining (1 Mark):**
**DNA Polymerase I** removes RNA primers using its 5′→3′ exonuclease activity and fills the gaps with DNA. **DNA Ligase** (NAD⁺-dependent in *E. coli*) seals the remaining nicks via phosphodiester bond formation.

#### Part (b) — Enzymes Involved (7 Marks)

| Enzyme | Function | Specific Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **DnaA** | Initiator protein; binds *oriC*; melts DUE | Requires ATP; origin recognition |
| **DnaB** | Main helicase; 5′→3′ translocation on lagging template | Hexameric ring; ATP-dependent |
| **DnaC** | Helicase loader | Releases DnaB onto ssDNA |
| **DnaG (Primase)** | Synthesises short RNA primers (10–12 nt) | Only RNA polymerase that makes primers for replication |
| **SSB Protein** | Binds and stabilises ssDNA; prevents re-annealing | Tetrameric; cooperative binding |
| **DNA Pol III (Holoenzyme)** | Main replicative polymerase; 5′→3′ synthesis + 3′→5′ proofreading | 10 subunits; ~1000 nt/s |
| **DNA Pol I** | Removes RNA primers (5′→3′ exo); fills gaps | Also has 3′→5′ proofreading |
| **DNA Ligase** | Seals nicks between Okazaki fragments | Uses NAD⁺ in *E. coli*; ATP in eukaryotes |
| **Topoisomerase (Gyrase)** | Relieves positive supercoils ahead of fork | Type II; ATP-dependent |
| **Tus Protein** | Replication terminator at Ter | Sequence-specific DNA-binding |

> **Mark Distribution (Part b):** 0.5–1 mark per enzyme × 7–8 enzymes with correct function.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Main Paper]**

**"Explain the Meselson–Stahl experiment in detail. How did it prove the semi-conservative nature of DNA replication? Discuss how replication initiates in eukaryotes."**

#### Part (a) — The Meselson–Stahl Experiment (7 Marks)

**Background (1 Mark):**
Three theoretical models of DNA replication were proposed:
1. **Conservative** — parental duplex preserved; entirely new duplex produced.
2. **Semi-conservative** — each daughter has one old + one new strand.
3. **Dispersive** — each daughter contains a mixture of old and new segments in each strand.

**Experimental Procedure (4 Marks):**
1. *E. coli* cells were grown for many generations in medium containing the heavy nitrogen isotope **$^{15}\text{N}$** (as $^{15}\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$). All cellular DNA became fully labelled with $^{15}\text{N}$, giving it a higher buoyant density of **1.717 g/cm³** in CsCl gradients.
2. The cells were then transferred to **$^{14}\text{N}$** medium and allowed to replicate.
3. Samples were withdrawn after **0, 1, 2, 3 generations**, and DNA was extracted.
4. Each sample was subjected to **CsCl density-gradient centrifugation** at ~100,000 × g for 20–24 h. The DNA bands at a position corresponding to its buoyant density.

**Observations and Conclusions (2 Marks):**

| Generation | Observed Bands | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (parental) | One band — heavy ($^{15}\text{N}/^{15}\text{N}$), 1.717 g/cm³ | Starting reference |
| 1 (after 1st replication) | **One band** — hybrid ($^{15}\text{N}/^{14}\text{N}$), 1.714 g/cm³ | Rules out conservative model |
| 2 (after 2nd replication) | **Two bands** — hybrid (1.714) + light (1.710) | Rules out dispersive model |
| 3 | Two bands — light band intensifies | Confirms semi-conservative model |

**Conclusion:** The hybrid band appearing at Generation 1 and splitting into hybrid + light at Generation 2 conclusively proved that **each daughter DNA duplex contains one parental strand and one newly synthesised strand — the semi-conservative model**.

#### Part (b) — Eukaryotic Replication Initiation (7 Marks)

Eukaryotic replication initiation is more complex than prokaryotic, reflecting the larger genome and chromatin structure.

**1. Origin Recognition (2 Marks):**
Eukaryotic origins of replication are called **ARS (Autonomously Replicating Sequences)** in yeast, or just **origins** in higher eukaryotes (where they are less well-defined). The **Origin Recognition Complex (ORC)** — a six-subunit protein complex (Orc1–Orc6) — binds to the origin throughout the cell cycle.

**2. Pre-Replicative Complex Assembly (2 Marks):**
During **G1 phase**, additional factors are recruited in a Cdc6- and Cdt1-dependent manner:
- **Cdc6** (Cell Division Cycle 6)
- **Cdt1** (Cdc10-dependent transcript 1)
- **MCM2–MCM7** helicase complex (the replicative helicase) is loaded as a double hexamer around ds-DNA in an inactive form.

This assembled state is called the **pre-replicative complex (pre-RC)** and licenses the origin for a single round of replication.

**3. Activation (2 Marks):**
On entry into **S-phase**, two key kinases are activated:
- **CDK (Cyclin-Dependent Kinase)** — drives cell-cycle progression.
- **DDK (Dbf4-dependent kinase)** — phosphorylates MCM.

These phosphorylations cause:
- MCM helicase double hexamers to separate and become active.
- Cdc6 and Cdt1 to be released and degraded (preventing re-replication).
- Additional factors (**MCM10, Cdc45, GINS**) assemble to form the **CMG complex (Cdc45-MCM-GINS)**, the active eukaryotic helicase moving 3′→5′.

**4. Polymerase Recruitment (1 Mark):**
- **Pol α-primase** synthesises a hybrid RNA-DNA primer (~20 nt).
- The primer is then handed off to **Pol ε** (leading strand) and **Pol δ** (lagging strand) for processive elongation.
- **RPA** (eukaryotic SSB) stabilises the ssDNA.

> **Mark Distribution (Part b):** ORC (1.5) + pre-RC (2) + Activation (2) + CMG + Pol recruitment (1.5).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in DNA Replication Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting directionality:** Marks are lost if students write that DNA polymerase synthesises in the 3′→5′ direction. The **correct direction is 5′→3′**; it reads the template 3′→5′. This single error can cost 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Pol I and Pol III:** Pol III does the main synthesis; Pol I only removes primers. Mixing these up is a frequent mistake.
> 3. **Missing the role of primase:** Some students omit that **primase is a RNA polymerase** that lays down a RNA primer — polymerase itself cannot start.
> 4. **Misnaming the lagging-strand fragments:** "Okazaki fragments" must be written in full at first mention; using only "fragments" loses a mark.
> 5. **Forgetting energy currency of ligase:** *E. coli* ligase uses **NAD⁺**, not ATP. Eukaryotic ligase uses **ATP**. Examiners love to test this distinction.
> 6. **Skipping the proofreading role:** Always mention the **3′→5′ exonuclease** activity of DNA polymerase for fidelity.
> 7. **In numericals — units:** Distinguish **nt (nucleotides)** from **bp (base pairs)** carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DNA replication is semi-conservative** (proved by Meselson & Stahl, 1958).
- Each parental strand serves as a **template** for the synthesis of a new complementary strand.
- DNA polymerase synthesises **only 5′→3′**, reading the template **3′→5′**.
- **Leading strand** = continuous; **Lagging strand** = discontinuous (Okazaki fragments).
- A short **RNA primer** is mandatory; primase (DnaG) synthesises it.
- **Helicase (DnaB)** unwinds the double helix using ATP.
- **SSB protein** prevents re-annealing of separated strands.
- **DNA Pol III** is the main replicative polymerase in prokaryotes; **Pol I** removes RNA primers.
- **DNA Ligase** seals the nicks (uses NAD⁺ in *E. coli*, ATP in eukaryotes).
- **Topoisomerase (Gyrase)** relieves superhelical tension ahead of the fork.
- Prokaryotes have **one origin** (*oriC*); eukaryotes have **multiple origins (ARS)**.
- Eukaryotes use **ORC → Cdc6/Cdt1 → MCM2–7 → CMG** for initiation.
- **Telomerase** (a reverse transcriptase) maintains chromosome ends in eukaryotes.
- Replication rate: **~1000 nt/s/fork** (prokaryotes); **~50 nt/s/fork** (eukaryotes).
- Okazaki fragment size: **~1–2 kb** (prokaryotes); **~100–200 nt** (eukaryotes).
- Final replication fidelity: $\sim 10^{-9}$ errors/base (after proofreading + mismatch repair).
- *E. coli* genome (4.6 Mbp) replicates in **~40 min**; human genome (~3.2 Gbp) in **~8 h**.
- In food biotechnology, replication underpins **starter culture growth, recombinant enzyme production, PCR-based food testing, and GMO detection**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transcription and Translation in Prokaryotes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT413 - FOOD BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transcription and Translation in Prokaryotes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Transcription in Prokaryotes — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transcription** is the enzyme-catalysed synthesis of a single-stranded RNA molecule (messenger RNA, mRNA) using one strand of the double-stranded DNA as a template. In prokaryotes, this process is carried out by a single multi-subunit enzyme called **RNA polymerase (RNAP)** — a ~400 kDa complex with the subunit composition $\alpha_2\beta\beta'\omega$ plus a $\sigma$ (sigma) factor that confers promoter specificity.

The reaction can be summarised as:

$$\text{DNA template} + n(\text{NTPs}) \xrightarrow{\text{RNA Polymerase, } \sigma^{70}} \text{mRNA} + n(\text{PP_i})$$

The pyrophosphate ($\text{PP}_i$) released is hydrolysed to $2\text{P}_i$, making the reaction effectively **irreversible** and energetically favourable.

### 1.2 Translation in Prokaryotes — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Translation** is the ribosome-mediated decoding of an mRNA sequence into a polypeptide chain. In prokaryotes, translation occurs simultaneously with transcription (co-transcriptional translation) because there is no nuclear membrane separating DNA from ribosomes. The ribosome (70S in prokaryotes, composed of 50S + 30S subunits) reads the mRNA in triplets called **codons** (5′→3′) and recruits the corresponding **aminoacyl-tRNAs** charged with specific amino acids.

$$\text{mRNA} + n(\text{aminoacyl-tRNA}) + n(\text{GTP}) \xrightarrow{\text{Ribosome (70S)}} \text{Polypeptide} + n(\text{GTP}) + n(\text{AMP/PP}_i)$$

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Bakery Blueprint Factory

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — From Master Recipe to Fresh Bread:**
> - Think of the **DNA double helix** as the *Master Cookbook* locked in the head office (nucleoid).
> - **Transcription** is the act of a chef (RNA polymerase) photocopying *one specific recipe* (a gene) onto a working slip (mRNA). The chef uses only one strand as the template — the *sense/coding strand* is what the copy looks like (same language, just RNA letters instead of DNA letters).
> - The working slip (mRNA) travels immediately to the bakery floor where bakers (ribosomes) read it. Each set of 3 letters (a **codon**) tells them which ingredient (amino acid) to add next.
> - The bakers use pre-loaded ingredient carts (**tRNAs**) that match each codon to the right amino acid.
> - The completed dough (polypeptide) is folded and sent out as a finished loaf (functional protein).

This **"DNA → mRNA → Protein"** flow is the celebrated **Central Dogma of Molecular Biology** (Crick, 1958, with revisions in 1970).

### 1.4 Key Quantitative Anchors (KTU High-Yield Numbers)

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Codon length | **3 nucleotides** | Triplet code |
| Number of sense codons | **61** | Encode 20 amino acids |
| Stop codons | **3** (UAA, UAG, UGA) | Translation termination |
| Universality of code | ~**97–98%** | Near-universal across life |
| Energy per amino acid added | **4 high-energy phosphate bonds** (2 ATP for aa-tRNA + 2 GTP on ribosome) | Metabolic cost of translation |
| Prokaryotic ribosome | **70S** (50S + 30S) | Sedimentation coefficient |
| Transcription rate (*E. coli*) | **40–80 nucleotides/sec** | Rapid bacterial growth |
| Translation rate (*E. coli*) | **15–20 amino acids/sec** | Coupled with transcription |
| RNA polymerase subunits | $\alpha_2\beta\beta'\omega\sigma$ | Holoenzyme composition |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Genetic Code — Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before studying the mechanics, every KTU answer must begin with the **properties of the genetic code** — examiners award dedicated marks for this preamble.

The genetic code is the dictionary that maps 64 possible codons to 20 amino acids + stop signals. Its salient properties are:

1. **Triplet** — every codon is exactly 3 nucleotides long.
2. **Universal** — almost identical from bacteria to humans (exceptions: mitochondria, *Mycoplasma*, ciliates).
3. **Degenerate / Redundant** — multiple codons specify the same amino acid (e.g., Leu has 6 codons).
4. **Non-overlapping** — codons are read in a fixed reading frame, one after another.
5. **Comma-less / No punctuation** — there are no gaps between codons; an in/del causes a **frameshift**.
6. **Start codon** — **AUG** (sometimes GUG/UUG in bacteria) codes for **N-formyl-methionine (fMet)** in prokaryotes.
7. **Stop codons** — **UAA (ochre), UAG (amber), UGA (opal/umber)** — bind release factors, not tRNAs.
8. **Wobble hypothesis (Crick, 1966)** — the 3rd base of a codon (5′→3′) pairs loosely with the 1st anticodon base, explaining degeneracy.

### 2.2 Transcription in Prokaryotes — Three-Phase Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> Prokaryotic transcription is **physically and temporally coupled** with translation — ribosomes begin translating an mRNA even before its 3′ end is synthesised. This is a **favourite KTU long-answer point**.

#### Phase A — Initiation

- The RNA polymerase holoenzyme ($\alpha_2\beta\beta'\omega\sigma^{70}$ in *E. coli*) binds to a **promoter** region.
- Two conserved consensus sequences are recognised:
  - **−10 box (Pribnow box):** TATAAT, located ~10 bp upstream of the transcription start site (+1).
  - **−35 box:** TTGACA, located ~35 bp upstream.
- The $\sigma^{70}$ factor recognises and binds the −35 and −10 elements.
- The DNA is **melted** (transcription bubble of ~12–14 bp) forming the **open complex**.
- The first NTP is loaded; once ~9–12 nt are polymerised, **$\sigma$ dissociates** and the polymerase enters the elongation mode.

#### Phase B — Elongation

- Core enzyme ($\alpha_2\beta\beta'\omega$) moves 3′→5′ along the **template (anti-sense) strand**.
- New ribonucleotides are added 5′→3′ to the growing mRNA, complementary to the template.
- The transcription bubble moves with the polymerase; DNA ahead is unwound, behind it rewinds.
- **Topological stress** (supercoiling) is relieved by **DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II)** and **topoisomerase I**.

#### Phase C — Termination

Two distinct mechanisms exist in *E. coli*:

**(i) $\rho$-dependent termination**
- The **Rho ($\rho$) protein** is a hexameric ATP-dependent helicase.
- It binds to the **rut site** (Rho utilisation site, ~70 nt of unstructured C-rich, G-poor RNA).
- Rho translocates 5′→3′ along the RNA, catches up with the paused polymerase, and **unwinds the RNA-DNA hybrid** using ATP hydrolysis, releasing the transcript.

**(ii) $\rho$-independent (intrinsic) termination**
- Requires a **GC-rich hairpin** (~7–9 bp stem, 3–4 nt loop) followed by a run of **uracils (UUUUUUU)** in the RNA.
- The hairpin pauses the polymerase; the weak rU–dA hybrid in the active site dissociates spontaneously.

### 2.3 Translation in Prokaryotes — Three-Phase Mechanism

#### Phase A — Initiation (requires IF-1, IF-2, IF-3, GTP)

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **30S subunit** | Binds mRNA at the **Shine-Dalgarno (SD) sequence** (AGGAGG) ~7 nt upstream of AUG |
| **IF-1** | Blocks A-site on 30S, prevents premature tRNA entry |
| **IF-3** | Prevents premature 50S joining; aids SD pairing |
| **IF-2** | GTP-bound protein that recruits **fMet-tRNA$^fMet$** into the P-site |
| **50S subunit** | Joins to form the 70S initiation complex; GTP hydrolysed |

Initiation codon in prokaryotes is **AUG** (sometimes GUG/UUG), but the **formyl group** on methionine is critical (added by **Met-tRNA transformylase** using $N^{10}$-formyl-THF).

#### Phase B — Elongation (requires EF-Tu, EF-Ts, EF-G)

- A new **aminoacyl-tRNA** is delivered to the **A-site** as a ternary complex: **EF-Tu·GTP·aa-tRNA**.
- Correct codon-anticodon pairing triggers **GTP hydrolysis**; **EF-Tu·GDP** released.
- **Peptidyl transferase** (50S subunit, rRNA-based **ribozyme**) catalyses peptide bond formation: the P-site polypeptide is transferred to the A-site amino acid.
- **Translocation**: **EF-G·GTP** pushes the ribosome one codon (3 nt) along the mRNA, shifting:
  - tRNA in A-site → P-site
  - tRNA in P-site → E-site (exits)
  - New codon exposed in A-site.

#### Phase C — Termination (requires RF1, RF2, RF3)

- A stop codon (UAA/UAG/UGA) enters the A-site.
- **RF1** recognises UAA/UAG; **RF2** recognises UAA/UGA.
- **RF3·GTP** facilitates release factor dissociation.
- **Peptidyl release** is catalysed (the ribozyme activity hydrolyses the peptidyl-tRNA bond).
- The **ribosome recycling factor (RRF)** and **EF-G** split the ribosome into 30S + 50S subunits.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises every quantitative relationship examiners can frame as a calculation. Memorise these — they appear in Part A and as sub-parts in Part B numericals.

| Concept | Equation / Identity | Typical Value (Prokaryote) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of codons | $4^3 = 64$ | 64 |
| Number of sense codons | $64 - 3 = 61$ | 61 |
| Degeneracy of an amino acid | $N_{codons}$ per aa | Leu=6, Met=1, Trp=1 |
| Polypeptide length from mRNA | $L_{aa} = (N_{nt} - 3U)/3$ | where $U$ = stop codons (1 in prokaryotes) |
| mRNA length needed for a peptide | $N_{nt} = 3 \cdot L_{aa} + 3$ | 3 nt for stop codon |
| Energy cost per amino acid | $4 \text{ high-energy phosphates}$ | 2 ATP equiv. for aa-tRNA + 2 GTP |
| Free energy of peptide bond | $\Delta G \approx -8$ to $-12$ kJ/mol | Spontaneous hydrolysis reverse |
| Peptide bond formation thermodynamics | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{peptide}} \approx +10$ to $+20$ kJ/mol | Driven by GTP hydrolysis |
| Transcription rate (*E. coli*) | $v_{tx} \approx 40$–$80$ nt/s | At 37 °C |
| Translation rate (*E. coli*) | $v_{tl} \approx 15$–$20$ aa/s | Coupled with transcription |
| mRNA half-life (prokaryotes) | $t_{1/2} \approx 2$–$5$ min | Very short — enables rapid response |
| ATP regeneration cost per codon | $4 \times$ NTP = 4 ATP equivalents | Per amino acid polymerised |
| Charging of tRNA cost | $1$ ATP $\rightarrow$ AMP + PP$_i$ | Equivalent to 2 ATP |

**Important numerical sign convention (KTU 2024)**
For "calculate the total energy cost of synthesising a protein of n amino acids":

$$E_{total} = 4n \quad \text{(ATP equivalents)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility for Food Biotechnology engineers:** The energy cost of protein synthesis is a key parameter in designing *cell factories* (e.g., recombinant *E. coli* producing chymosin for cheese-making or industrial enzymes). Reducing wasted protein over-expression directly improves bio-process economics. The coupling of transcription–translation in prokaryotes is also exploited in **operon-based expression systems** (e.g., the *lac* operon) used to produce food-grade enzymes and recombinant probiotics.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Transcription vs. Translation (Prokaryotes)

| Dimension | Transcription | Translation |
|---|---|---|
| Template | DNA (one strand) | mRNA |
| Enzyme | RNA polymerase (core + σ) | Ribozyme (rRNA in 50S) |
| Building blocks | NTPs (ATP, GTP, CTP, UTP) | Aminoacyl-tRNAs |
| Direction of synthesis | 5′→3′ | N→C terminus |
| Start signal | −10 / −35 promoter (Pribnow) | Shine-Dalgarno + AUG |
| Stop signal | ρ / hairpin-U | UAA / UAG / UGA |
| Energy per addition | 2 phosphoanhydride bonds (NTP→NMP + PP$_i$) | 4 high-energy bonds (2 ATP for charging + 2 GTP on ribosome) |
| Location | Cytoplasm (nucleoid) | Cytoplasm (70S ribosome) |
| Coupling in prokaryotes | Coupled to translation | Coupled to transcription |
| Product | RNA (mRNA, tRNA, rRNA) | Polypeptide |

### 3.2 Derivation — Codon-to-Amino-Acid Mapping Mathematics

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often ask: *"Given an mRNA sequence, determine the polypeptide produced."* The derivation below shows the formal method.

**Given:** an mRNA sequence of length $N_{nt}$ nucleotides.

**Step 1 — Identify the reading frame.** Search for the **start codon (AUG)** in the 5′ UTR using the **Shine-Dalgarno rule** (in prokaryotes): look for the complement of 5′-AGGAGG-3′ located ~5–10 nt upstream of the start codon.

**Step 2 — Read the codons.** Starting at the A of AUG, partition the downstream sequence into non-overlapping triplets:

$$\text{Codon}_i = \{n_{3i+1}, n_{3i+2}, n_{3i+3}\}, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

**Step 3 — Translate each codon** using the standard codon table.

**Step 4 — Terminate at the first in-frame stop codon** (UAA, UAG, UGA).

**Step 5 — Calculate the polypeptide length:**

$$L_{aa} = \frac{N_{nt}^{ORF} - 3}{3}$$

where $N_{nt}^{ORF}$ is the length of the open reading frame from start (A) of AUG to last nucleotide of the stop codon (inclusive).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Derivation — Energy Cost of a Recombinant Protein

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A frequent KTU-style numerical:** *"Calculate the total ATP equivalents consumed by an *E. coli* cell to synthesise a single molecule of recombinant chymosin (a 323-amino-acid protein used in cheese manufacture). Include the cost of: (i) transcription, (ii) translation, (iii) amino-acid charging, and (iv) mRNA turnover."*

#### Step 1 — Number of mRNA nucleotides required

The mRNA must encode 323 amino acids + 1 stop codon:

$$N_{nt} = 3 \cdot L_{aa} + 3 = 3 \cdot 323 + 3 = 969 + 3 = 972 \text{ nt}$$

#### Step 2 — Energy cost of transcription

Each NTP incorporated is derived from an NTP; the pyrophosphate released is hydrolysed to 2 P$_i$. Conventionally, this is counted as **2 high-energy phosphate bonds per nucleotide** (NTP → NMP + PP$_i$, then PP$_i$ + H$_2$O → 2 P$_i$).

$$E_{tx} = 2 \cdot N_{nt} = 2 \cdot 972 = 1944 \text{ ATP equivalents}$$

#### Step 3 — Energy cost of translation

Each peptide bond consumes **4 high-energy phosphates** (2 ATP for tRNA charging + 2 GTP on the ribosome: 1 for EF-Tu delivery, 1 for EF-G translocation).

$$E_{tl} = 4 \cdot L_{aa} = 4 \cdot 323 = 1292 \text{ ATP equivalents}$$

#### Step 4 — Energy cost of fMet-tRNA charging (initiator methionine)

The first amino acid is **N-formyl-methionine**, charged by methionyl-tRNA formyltransferase using $N^{10}$-formyl-THF (which itself is derived from ATP/folate metabolism). Conventionally count it as **2 ATP equivalents** (same as a normal Met-tRNA charging, 1 ATP → AMP + PP$_i$).

$$E_{fMet} = 2 \text{ ATP equivalents}$$

#### Step 5 — Energy cost of mRNA turnover (degradation)

Prokaryotic mRNAs are degraded by **RNase E, RNase III, PNPase** in a multiprotein degradosome complex. Each phosphodiester bond cleaved releases a nucleoside 5′-monophosphate (NMP), not NTP, so degradation does **not** directly recover high-energy bonds.

However, salvage pathways resynthesise NTPs: NMP + ATP → NDP + ADP (monophosphate kinase, AMP kinase) and NDP + ATP → NTP + ADP (nucleoside diphosphate kinase). Each recycled NMP costs **2 ATP equivalents**.

$$E_{deg} = 2 \cdot N_{nt} = 2 \cdot 972 = 1944 \text{ ATP equivalents}$$

> Note: Some texts include transcription cost in turnover since the mRNA will be re-transcribed. The convention used here is conservative.

#### Step 6 — Total energy cost

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= E_{tx} + E_{tl} + E_{fMet} + E_{deg} \\
&= 1944 + 1292 + 2 + 1944 \\
&= 5182 \text{ ATP equivalents}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 7 — Engineering interpretation

If a fermenter produces **1 g/L of chymosin** (M$_r$ ≈ 35 600 Da) at biomass density $X$ = 50 g DCW/L, the specific productivity translates to an enormous ATP demand — this is why industrial *E. coli* cultures are **fed-batch with glycerol/glucose** to keep ATP pools replenished. The energy cost calculation above is a standard input to **metabolic flux analysis (MFA)** in food-biotech process design.

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation — Polypeptide Length from mRNA Size

**Given:** mRNA length = 1500 nt (excluding the 5′ UTR, poly-A tail, etc.)  
**Find:** Maximum polypeptide length

**Step 1 — Subtract start and stop codons:**

$$N_{coding} = 1500 - 3 = 1497 \text{ nt}$$

**Step 2 — Divide by 3:**

$$L_{aa}^{max} = \frac{1497}{3} = 499 \text{ amino acids}$$

> Note: This is a *maximum*; actual length depends on the position of the first in-frame stop codon (which may be earlier than the physical 3′ end).

### 3.5 Analytical Derivation — Wobble Position and Degeneracy

The **wobble hypothesis** (Crick, 1966) states that the 5′ anticodon base (position 34 of the tRNA) can pair with more than one 3′ codon base. Standard pairing rules at the wobble position:

| 5′ Anticodon base | Pairs with 3′ codon base |
|---|---|
| G | C or U |
| U | A or G |
| I (inosine) | A, U, or C |
| C | G only |

This is why families of tRNAs (isoacceptors) can read multiple codons for the same amino acid — a **direct molecular explanation for the degeneracy of the genetic code**.

The minimum number of tRNAs needed to read all 61 sense codons is **~31** (with inosine at the wobble position, this is sufficient).

$$N_{tRNA}^{min} = 31 \quad (\text{in } E. coli, \text{ there are } \sim 86 \text{ tRNA genes for } \sim 44 \text{ isoacceptors})$$

> **Why this matters for Food Biotechnology:** The *lac* operon (lactose utilisation), *ara* operon (arabinose), and *mel* operon (melibiose) in food-grade *Lactococcus*, *Lactobacillus*, and recombinant *E. coli* are all classic poly-cistronic mRNA units. Coupled transcription–translation is what makes rapid sugar-switching metabolism possible in dairy starter cultures.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define the genetic code. List any four salient properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The genetic code is the set of rules by which the 64 possible triplet codons of mRNA are translated into the 20 standard amino acids.

Four salient properties:

1. **Triplet and non-overlapping** — each codon is 3 nucleotides, read sequentially without overlap.
2. **Degenerate / redundant** — multiple codons specify the same amino acid (e.g., 6 codons for leucine).
3. **Universal** — nearly identical from bacteria to mammals, with minor exceptions in mitochondria.
4. **Comma-less** — there is no gap or punctuation between codons; the ribosome reads them continuously.

*(Other acceptable properties for full credit: start codon specificity, stop codons, wobble, non-ambiguity.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between $\rho$-dependent and $\rho$-independent termination of transcription in prokaryotes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | $\rho$-dependent | $\rho$-independent |
|---|---|---|
| Termination factor | Rho ($\rho$) hexameric ATPase | No protein factor; intrinsic to RNA |
| Signal in mRNA | **rut site** (C-rich, G-poor, ~70 nt, unstructured) | **GC-rich hairpin** + downstream **poly-U** tract |
| Energy source | ATP hydrolysis | Conformational energy of hairpin + weak rU–dA hybrid |
| Genetic evidence | Inhibited by *rho* mutations (*rho-* mutants read through) | Functions in protein-free in vitro systems |
| Examples | *trp*, *lac* in some conditions | *trp attenuator*, most *E. coli* terminators |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part-B question carries 14 marks and typically offers internal choice. Below are **two fully-worked alternatives** of equal cognitive demand.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the **three phases of transcription in prokaryotes** with suitable diagrams. Highlight the role of the $\sigma$ factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of translation elongation** in prokaryotes, including the roles of EF-Tu, EF-G, and the peptidyl transferase centre. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Phases of Transcription (7 marks)

**Initiation (3 marks)**

- The **RNA polymerase holoenzyme** in *E. coli* is $\alpha_2\beta\beta'\omega\sigma^{70}$.
- The $\sigma^{70}$ factor scans the genome and recognises two conserved promoter elements:
  - **−35 box (TTGACA)** — initial recognition.
  - **−10 box / Pribnow box (TATAAT)** — melting of the DNA.
- After binding, the DNA around the −10 region is **melted over ~12–14 bp** to form the **open complex** (also called the transcription bubble).
- The first few NTPs are added in a process called **abortive initiation** (polymerase synthesises 2–9 nt RNAs and releases them).
- When the RNA reaches ~9–12 nt, the $\sigma$ factor **dissociates**, leaving the **core enzyme** ($\alpha_2\beta\beta'\omega$) to enter elongation.

**Role of $\sigma$ factor (1 mark, embedded)**
- Imparts **promoter specificity** (without $\sigma$, core enzyme transcribes non-specifically).
- Aids DNA melting at the −10 element.
- Different $\sigma$ factors (e.g., $\sigma^{32}$ for heat-shock, $\sigma^{54}$ for nitrogen) redirect transcription to alternative regulons.

**Elongation (2 marks)**

- The core enzyme moves **3′→5′ along the template (anti-sense) strand**.
- New ribonucleotides complementary to the template are added to the **3′-OH of the growing mRNA** (5′→3′ synthesis).
- The transcription bubble moves with the enzyme; **DNA gyrase** (topoisomerase II) and **topoisomerase I** manage supercoiling ahead of and behind the polymerase.
- In prokaryotes, **ribosomes begin translation co-transcriptionally** before the mRNA is complete.

**Termination (2 marks)**

- **$\rho$-dependent:** Rho binds the *rut* site, translocates 5′→3′ using ATP, unwinds the RNA–DNA hybrid, and releases the transcript.
- **$\rho$-independent (intrinsic):** A GC-rich hairpin followed by a poly-U tract forms. The hairpin pauses the polymerase, and the weak rU–dA hybrid in the active centre dissociates spontaneously, releasing the transcript.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Translation Elongation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Delivery of aminoacyl-tRNA to the A-site (2 marks)**

- A new aminoacyl-tRNA is escorted to the A-site as a **ternary complex**: **EF-Tu·GTP·aa-tRNA**.
- EF-Tu is a GTP-binding protein; in its GTP-bound form it has high affinity for aminoacylated tRNAs.
- The anticodon of the tRNA base-pairs with the mRNA codon in the A-site.
- **Proofreading:** correct codon–anticodon pairing triggers GTP hydrolysis on EF-Tu; **EF-Tu·GDP** is released and recycled by **EF-Ts** (GDP → GTP exchange).

**Step 2 — Peptide bond formation (3 marks)**

- Catalysed by the **peptidyl transferase centre (PTC)**, located in the **50S subunit**.
- The catalytic activity resides in the **23S rRNA** (a **ribozyme**, not a protein enzyme).
- The $\alpha$-amino group of the A-site amino acid performs a **nucleophilic attack** on the carbonyl carbon of the P-site peptidyl-tRNA ester bond.
- The result: the polypeptide chain is transferred to the A-site tRNA, leaving a **deacylated tRNA** in the P-site.

**Step 3 — Translocation (2 marks)**

- **EF-G·GTP** binds the ribosome and uses the energy of GTP hydrolysis to translocate the ribosome by **exactly 3 nucleotides (one codon)** along the mRNA.
- After translocation:
  - The A-site tRNA (now carrying the elongated peptide) shifts to the **P-site**.
  - The P-site deacylated tRNA shifts to the **E-site** (exit) and dissociates.
  - The A-site is vacant and ready for the next aminoacyl-tRNA·EF-Tu·GTP ternary complex.

**Energy accounting (embedded)**
- 2 GTP per elongation cycle (1 for EF-Tu delivery, 1 for EF-G translocation) + 2 ATP for tRNA charging = **4 high-energy phosphates per amino acid**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the **properties of the genetic code** and describe the **wobble hypothesis**. How does wobble explain the degeneracy of the code? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **initiation of translation in prokaryotes** with a neat labelled diagram. Mention the role of **Shine-Dalgarno sequence** and **initiation factors IF1, IF2, IF3**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Genetic Code & Wobble (7 marks)

**Properties of the Genetic Code (4 marks)**

1. **Triplet:** 3 nucleotides = 1 codon → $4^3 = 64$ codons.
2. **Degenerate (redundant):** 61 codons code for 20 amino acids; e.g., Leu, Arg, Ser have 6 codons each; Met and Trp have 1.
3. **Universal:** Nearly identical across all organisms. Minor exceptions include mitochondrial code (e.g., UGA = Trp, not stop) and *Mycoplasma* (use UGA for Trp).
4. **Non-overlapping and comma-less:** Each nucleotide belongs to only one codon; no gaps between codons.
5. **Non-ambiguous:** Each codon specifies only **one** amino acid (the reverse is not true — this is what "degeneracy" means).
6. **Polarity:** Codons are read 5′→3′ with a fixed direction.

**Wobble Hypothesis — Crick (1966) (3 marks)**

- The pairing between the **3rd base of the codon (5′→3′)** and the **1st base of the anticodon (5′→3′)** is **less stringent** than Watson-Crick pairing.
- The 1st anticodon base (also called the **wobble position**) can be:
  - **G** — pairs with C or U
  - **U** — pairs with A or G
  - **I (inosine)** — pairs with A, U, or C
  - **C** — pairs with G only

**How Wobble Explains Degeneracy**

- A single tRNA bearing, for example, inosine at the wobble position can read three different codons in the same family (e.g., the Phe tRNA with anticodon 3′-AAI-5′ reads both UUU and UUC).
- Hence, the **minimum number of tRNAs needed to decode all 61 sense codons is ~31**, not 61.
- The degeneracy of the code is therefore an **evolutionary optimisation**: it minimises the tRNA gene pool while tolerating third-position mutations as silent (synonymous) substitutions.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Initiation of Translation in Prokaryotes (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Formation of the 30S pre-initiation complex (2 marks)**

- The **30S ribosomal subunit** binds the mRNA at the **Shine-Dalgarno (SD) sequence** — a purine-rich motif (consensus: 5′-AGGAGG-3′) located 5–10 nt **upstream** of the AUG start codon.
- Base-pairing occurs between the SD sequence and the **3′ anti-SD end of the 16S rRNA** (3′-UCCUCC-5′) within the 30S subunit.
- **IF-3** binds the 30S subunit to **prevent premature association of the 50S subunit** and to promote SD pairing fidelity.
- **IF-1** binds the **A-site of the 30S** to block premature tRNA entry.

**Step 2 — Recruitment of fMet-tRNA$^fMet$ (2 marks)**

- **IF-2**, a GTP-binding protein, escorts the **initiator aminoacyl-tRNA** — specifically **N-formyl-methionyl-tRNA$^fMet$** — into the **P-site** of the 30S subunit.
- The methionine is **formylated** by **Met-tRNA transformylase** using $N^{10}$-formyl-tetrahydrofolate; this is what distinguishes the prokaryotic initiator tRNA from the elongator Met-tRNA$^mMet$.
- The anticodon of fMet-tRNA$^fMet$ base-pairs with the AUG codon in the P-site.

**Step 3 — Formation of the 70S initiation complex (2 marks)**

- The **50S subunit** joins the complex, forming the **70S initiation complex**.
- Joining triggers **GTP hydrolysis on IF-2**; the energy released drives the conformational rearrangement that expels IF-1, IF-2, and IF-3.
- The initiation complex is now competent for the **first peptide bond** to be formed upon arrival of the second aminoacyl-tRNA in the A-site.

**Step 4 — Energy cost of initiation (1 mark)**

- 1 GTP (on IF-2) + 1 ATP (formylation) + 1 ATP (charging) = **3 high-energy phosphates** for initiation, on top of the per-amino-acid elongation cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not confuse Shine-Dalgarno (prokaryote) with Kozak sequence (eukaryote).** KTU examiners *love* setting this comparison — a 3-mark difference-of-one-word question.
> 2. **State the direction of synthesis explicitly:** transcription and translation both proceed **5′→3′**, with the template read **3′→5′**.
> 3. **"$\sigma$ factor dissociates"** — be specific; it dissociates **after** the polymerase has synthesised ~9–12 nt (i.e., it is NOT released at the moment of promoter binding).
> 4. **Peptidyl transferase is a ribozyme (23S rRNA), not a protein enzyme.** This is a frequently tested "stumper".
> 5. **Energy cost per amino acid = 4 high-energy phosphates (2 ATP for charging + 2 GTP on ribosome).** Many students write "2 ATP" and lose 1 mark.
> 6. **In prokaryotes, transcription and translation are coupled** (no nucleus). In eukaryotes, they are spatially and temporally separated. State this in every long answer.
> 7. **Wobble position is the 1st base of the anticodon (5′ end of the anticodon, 3′ end of the codon).** Drawing the orientation wrong loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.

- [ ] **Central Dogma:** DNA $\xrightarrow{\text{transcription}}$ mRNA $\xrightarrow{\text{translation}}$ Protein. (Prokaryotes: coupled; Eukaryotes: separated.)
- [ ] **RNA polymerase (prokaryote):** $\alpha_2\beta\beta'\omega\sigma^{70}$. $\sigma$ confers promoter specificity.
- [ ] **Promoter elements:** −35 (TTGACA) and −10 (TATAAT, Pribnow box). +1 is the TSS.
- [ ] **Transcription direction:** 5′→3′ on mRNA; template read 3′→5′.
- [ ] **Termination:** $\rho$-dependent (Rho ATPase, *rut* site) and $\rho$-independent (GC hairpin + poly-U).
- [ ] **Genetic code:** triplet, degenerate, universal, non-overlapping, comma-less, non-ambiguous.
- [ ] **Start codon (prokaryote):** AUG → **N-formyl-methionine (fMet)**.
- [ ] **Stop codons:** UAA, UAG, UGA — no tRNAs; recognised by **RF1** (UAA, UAG) and **RF2** (UAA, UGA).
- [ ] **Wobble:** 1st anticodon base (I, G, U, C) pairs loosely with 3rd codon base; explains degeneracy.
- [ ] **Translation initiation (prokaryote):** 30S binds mRNA at **Shine-Dalgarno (AGGAGG)**; IF-1, IF-2 (GTP), IF-3; 50S joins → **70S initiation complex**; initiator = fMet-tRNA$^fMet$.
- [ ] **Elongation factors:** **EF-Tu·GTP** delivers aa-tRNA to A-site; **EF-Ts** recycles EF-Tu; **EF-G·GTP** translocates the ribosome.
- [ ] **Peptidyl transferase** = **23S rRNA ribozyme** (NOT a protein enzyme).
- [ ] **Energy cost per amino acid:** **4 high-energy phosphates** (2 ATP for charging + 2 GTP on ribosome).
- [ ] **Coupled transcription–translation in prokaryotes:** ribosomes attach to the 5′ end of mRNA even before its 3′ end is transcribed. This is **not** true in eukaryotes.
- [ ] **Polypeptide length from ORF:** $L_{aa} = (N_{nt} - 3)/3$.
- [ ] **Polycistronic mRNA in prokaryotes:** a single mRNA carries multiple ORFs (operon model: *lac*, *trp*, *ara*).
- [ ] **Ribosome recycling:** RRF + EF-G split the 70S ribosome.
- [ ] **Industrial relevance:** energy cost of protein over-expression, operon-based expression systems, and co-transcriptional translation are key levers in food-biotech process design (recombinant enzymes, probiotics, dairy cultures).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Enzymes in Bakery, Meat, Fruit, Vegetable and Dairy Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT413 - FOOD BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of Enzymes in Bakery, Meat, Fruit, Vegetable and Dairy Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Food-grade enzymes** are biocatalysts (typically proteins, occasionally ribozymes) of high molecular weight that accelerate specific biochemical reactions in food matrices under mild conditions of temperature, pH, and pressure without being consumed in the reaction. In the context of the KTU 2024 Food Biotechnology syllabus, the *application of enzymes* refers to the deliberate, controlled deployment of hydrolytic, oxidoreductive, or transferase-class enzymes to achieve desired textural, nutritional, flavor, or shelf-life modifications in five major food sub-sectors: **Bakery, Meat, Fruit, Vegetable, and Dairy industries**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Enzymes used in food processing are classified as **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** under the U.S. FDA / FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) regulatory framework. They are used in **microgram to milligram per kilogram** concentrations.

### Classification Basis
Food enzymes are systematically classified by the **IUBMB (International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology)** system into 6 major classes, each denoted by an **EC (Enzyme Commission) number**:

| EC Class | Reaction Type | Food Industry Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| EC 1 | Oxidoreductases | Glucose oxidase (Bakery) |
| EC 2 | Transferases | Transglutaminase (Meat) |
| EC 3 | Hydrolases | Amylase, Protease, Lipase |
| EC 4 | Lyases | Pectate lyase (Fruit) |
| EC 5 | Isomerases | Glucose isomerase |
| EC 6 | Ligases | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Nearly **80% of all industrial food enzymes** belong to **EC Class 3 (Hydrolases)** because hydrolytic reactions are the most practically relevant for food texture and flavor modification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Molecular "Scissors and Glue"

Imagine a freshly baked loaf of bread. The wheat flour contains long, tangled chains of starch and gluten proteins. Without help, water, heat, and time will eventually soften them, but the process is slow and inconsistent. Now, picture enzymes as **microscopic, highly specialized scissors and glue**:

- **Scissors (Hydrolases)** — cut long starch or protein chains into smaller, sweeter, more digestible pieces.
- **Glue (Transferases like Transglutaminase)** — bind small protein fragments together into a stronger, juicier network in meat.
- **Polishers (Oxidoreductases)** — bleach dough, strengthen gluten, and improve crumb whiteness.

Just as a tailor uses different tools to cut, stitch, and finish fabric, a food technologist uses specific enzymes to **cut, link, and remodel** the biopolymers of food.

## 1.3 Why Are Enzymes Preferred Over Chemical Additives?

1. **Specificity** — act on a single substrate, leaving other components untouched.
2. **Mild operating conditions** — typically 30 °C to 70 °C and pH 4 to 7 (saves energy).
3. **Eco-friendly / Green chemistry** — biodegradable, no toxic residues.
4. **Catalytic efficiency** — typical turnover numbers $k_{cat}$ range from **10 to 10⁷ s⁻¹**.
5. **Consumer acceptance** — perceived as "natural" processing aids.

## 1.4 Historical Context (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The first industrial food enzyme was **calf rennet (chymosin, EC 3.4.23.4)** used in cheese-making, dating back to **6000 BCE**. The first **microbial rennet from Mucor miehei** was commercialized in the 1960s, revolutionizing the dairy industry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Michaelis–Menten Framework (Theoretical Backbone)

Almost all quantitative enzyme problems in KTU examinations reduce to the **Michaelis–Menten equation**, which describes the initial reaction velocity $v_0$ as a function of substrate concentration $[S]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_0 = \frac{V_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $v_0$ = initial reaction velocity (mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹)
- $V_{max}$ = maximum reaction velocity when enzyme is saturated
- $K_m$ = Michaelis constant = substrate concentration at $v_0 = \frac{V_{max}}{2}$ (mol·L⁻¹)
- $[S]$ = substrate concentration (mol·L⁻¹)

### 2.1.1 Key Operating Regimes

- **First-order regime** ($[S] \ll K_m$): $v_0 \approx \frac{V_{max}}{K_m} \cdot [S]$ — reaction rate is directly proportional to substrate.
- **Zero-order regime** ($[S] \gg K_m$): $v_0 \approx V_{max}$ — enzyme is saturated, rate is independent of $[S]$.

> [!NOTE]
> In industrial food processing, enzymes are deliberately used in **excess** to push reactions toward the **zero-order regime** for consistent, time-controlled product quality.

### 2.1.2 The Lineweaver–Burk Double-Reciprocal Form

Used in KTU numerical problems to determine $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ experimentally:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{v_0} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}
\end{aligned}$$

A plot of $\frac{1}{v_0}$ vs. $\frac{1}{[S]}$ gives:
- **y-intercept** $= \frac{1}{V_{max}}$
- **x-intercept** $= -\frac{1}{K_m}$
- **slope** $= \frac{K_m}{V_{max}}$

## 2.2 Enzyme Activity Unit Definitions (KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|:---|:---|:---|
| International Unit | **1 U** | Amount of enzyme that catalyzes conversion of **1 μmol substrate per minute** at optimal conditions |
| Katal | **1 kat** | Amount of enzyme catalyzing **1 mol s⁻¹** of substrate |
| Specific activity | U·mg⁻¹ | Enzyme units per mg of total protein (purity indicator) |
| Turnover number | $k_{cat}$ | Moles of substrate converted per mole of enzyme per second |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion:** $1~\text{kat} = 6 \times 10^{7}~\text{U}$ (a frequently asked KTU conversion question!)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Industrial Use |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Michaelis–Menten | $v_0 = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Predict enzyme dose vs. reaction rate |
| Lineweaver–Burk | $\frac{1}{v_0} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}$ | Plot to find $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ |
| Arrhenius (T-dependence) | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Predict enzyme deactivation during baking |
| Q₁₀ rule | $Q_{10} = \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Rate change per 10 °C rise |
| Enzyme dose | $\text{Dose} = \frac{\text{Required Activity (U)}}{\text{Specific Activity (U·mg}^{-1}\text{)}}$ | Calculate enzyme addition in bakery / dairy |
| Lactose hydrolysis % | $\%~\text{Hydrolysis} = \frac{[\text{Glucose}]_{formed}}{[\text{Lactose}]_{initial}} \times 100$ | Dairy: lactase efficiency |
| Softening index | $\Delta_{soft} = \frac{F_{initial} - F_{treated}}{F_{initial}} \times 100$ | Fruit: pectinase effect on firmness |

> [!NOTE]
> **$Q_{10}$ (Temperature Coefficient):** Most food enzymes have a $Q_{10}$ value of approximately **1.5 to 3.0**, meaning the reaction rate roughly doubles for every 10 °C rise in temperature (within the active range).

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

The Michaelis–Menten framework allows a process engineer to:
1. **Dose optimization** — avoid over-dosing (waste) or under-dosing (slow reaction).
2. **Process time calculation** — $t = \frac{[S]_0 - [S]_t}{v_0}$.
3. **Scale-up** — from lab ($V = 1~\text{L}$) to industrial reactors ($V = 10^{4}~\text{L}$).
4. **Cost engineering** — minimize enzyme cost, which is typically **0.5–3% of total product cost**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Michaelis–Menten Equation

This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU derivation question**. We must derive it from the **Briggs–Haldane steady-state assumption**.

### Step 1 — Define the elementary reaction

The enzyme $E$ binds substrate $S$ to form the enzyme–substrate complex $ES$, which then breaks down into product $P$ and free enzyme $E$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E + S \;\underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; ES \;\overset{k_{2}}{\longrightarrow}\; E + P
\end{aligned}$$

Where $k_1$ is the association rate constant (M⁻¹·s⁻¹), $k_{-1}$ is the dissociation rate constant (s⁻¹), and $k_2$ is the catalytic rate constant (s⁻¹).

### Step 2 — Apply the steady-state assumption to [ES]

The Briggs–Haldane assumption states that the concentration of the $ES$ complex remains approximately constant over the reaction time scale:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1[E][S] - k_{-1}[ES] - k_2[ES] \;\approx\; 0
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **core postulate** that converts a transient kinetic problem into an algebraic one.

### Step 3 — Solve for [ES] in terms of total enzyme [E]₀

Rearranging the steady-state equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
k_1[E][S] &= (k_{-1} + k_2)[ES] \\[4pt]
[ES] &= \frac{k_1[E][S]}{k_{-1} + k_2}
\end{aligned}$$

Define the **Michaelis constant**:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}
\end{aligned}$$

So:

$$\begin{aligned}
[ES] = \frac{[E][S]}{K_m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Apply enzyme mass balance

Total enzyme is conserved:

$$\begin{aligned}
[E]_0 = [E] + [ES]
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $[E] = [E]_0 - [ES]$ into the previous result:

$$\begin{aligned}
[ES] = \frac{([E]_0 - [ES])[S]}{K_m}
\end{aligned}$$

Expand and collect $[ES]$ terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_m[ES] &= [E]_0[S] - [ES][S] \\[4pt]
[ES](K_m + [S]) &= [E]_0[S] \\[4pt]
[ES] &= \frac{[E]_0[S]}{K_m + [S]}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Define the initial reaction velocity

The rate of product formation is governed by the breakdown of $ES$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_0 = k_2[ES] = \frac{k_2[E]_0[S]}{K_m + [S]}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Recognize $V_{max}$

When $[S] \to \infty$, all enzyme is in the $ES$ form, so $[ES] = [E]_0$ and the velocity reaches its maximum:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{max} = k_2[E]_0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Final Michaelis–Menten equation

Substituting $V_{max}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,v_0 = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special case:** When $v_0 = \frac{V_{max}}{2}$, we get $[S] = K_m$. This is why $K_m$ is operationally defined as the substrate concentration at half-maximal velocity.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Lineweaver–Burk Equation

Starting from the Michaelis–Menten equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_0 &= \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}
\end{aligned}$$

Take the reciprocal of both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{v_0} &= \frac{K_m + [S]}{V_{max}[S]}
\end{aligned}$$

Split the right-hand side into two fractions:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{v_0} &= \frac{K_m}{V_{max}[S]} + \frac{[S]}{V_{max}[S]}
\end{aligned}$$

Cancel $[S]$ in the second term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,\frac{1}{v_0} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

This is a linear equation of the form $y = mx + c$ where:
- $y = \frac{1}{v_0}$
- $x = \frac{1}{[S]}$
- slope $m = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}}$
- y-intercept $c = \frac{1}{V_{max}}$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** In a bakery amylase assay, the following data were obtained at 30 °C:

| $[S]$ (mM) | $v_0$ (μmol·min⁻¹) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1.0 | 0.50 |
| 2.0 | 0.83 |
| 5.0 | 1.25 |
| 10.0 | 1.43 |

**Find $V_{max}$ and $K_m$.**

**Step 1 — Convert to reciprocals:**

| $\frac{1}{[S]}$ (mM⁻¹) | $\frac{1}{v_0}$ (min·μmol⁻¹) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 1.000 | 2.000 |
| 0.500 | 1.205 |
| 0.200 | 0.800 |
| 0.100 | 0.699 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression (slope and intercept):**

Using the formula for slope:

$$\begin{aligned}
m = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \approx 1.30~\text{min·mM·μmol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

And y-intercept:

$$\begin{aligned}
c = \frac{1}{V_{max}} \approx 0.70~\text{min·μmol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{max}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{max} = \frac{1}{c} = \frac{1}{0.70} \approx 1.43~\text{μmol·min}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $K_m$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_m = m \cdot V_{max} = 1.30 \times 1.43 \approx 1.86~\text{mM}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $K_m$ value is typical for **fungal α-amylase** used in bread-making. Lower $K_m$ indicates higher enzyme–substrate affinity, which is desirable for low-dose industrial application.

## 3.4 Arrhenius Derivation of Enzyme Thermal Inactivation

The thermal denaturation of enzymes follows first-order kinetics. The inactivation rate constant $k_d$ varies with absolute temperature $T$ (Kelvin) according to Arrhenius:

$$\begin{aligned}
k_d = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $E_a$ = activation energy for denaturation (typically 200–400 kJ·mol⁻¹ for proteins)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor)
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin

**Industrial Implication:** In a bakery oven at 100 °C (= 373 K), most amylases are rapidly inactivated. This is intentional — the enzyme must act during dough resting but not during baking.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
